sectionOrderRules: Hero must communicate accommodation type, place or setting if known, and a primary booking CTA.
Accommodation-summary should quickly explain who the stay suits and what makes the property practically relevant, not only atmospheric copy.
Room-highlights should preview a small number of concrete room options or stay formats and link to rum-och-priser.
Trust-proof must appear before the final booking CTA and may use reviews, policies or included amenities without repeating identical claims.
Location-preview should only appear when there is meaningful nearby context such as city center, nature, beach, station or attractions.
Sections should balance inspiration with practical booking information.
Rooms-intro must explain how room categories, occupancy or pricing work in plain language.
Room-list items must be concrete and distinguish rooms by size, bed setup, amenities, view or suitability.
Pricing information must be honest: use actual prices, from-prices or clear price examples when exact live pricing is unavailable.
If policies such as breakfast, cancellation or check-in affect the offer, they should appear near pricing or CTA rather than being buried.
Booking-intro must state whether the visitor books directly, requests availability or is redirected to an external booking flow.
Booking-cta must be the main interaction on the page and should not be visually buried below long text.
Booking-policies should cover the practical conditions supplied by the business, such as cancellation terms, check-in windows or breakfast inclusion, without inventing guarantees.
If a contact alternative is shown, it should support the booking flow rather than compete with it.
Gallery images should be grouped or captioned so visitors understand whether they show rooms, common areas, food or surroundings.
The page should stay visually focused and avoid turning into a duplicate of the home page.
A booking CTA must appear after visitors have seen enough imagery to act.
About-story must be specific to the property, host, building or local setting and avoid generic hospitality clichés.
Host-or-team should only be shown when real people or roles can be presented credibly.
Trust-proof may include guest reviews, practical standards or longstanding local presence, but must not imply unavailable awards or certifications.
Contact-info must include real phone, email and address details when available and must not use placeholders.
Map should support wayfinding to the property, not replace the written address.
If check-in times, reception times or arrival notes matter, they should be placed near the contact details.
